title: Learning Diary
text:
Introduction
My name is Beatriz Werneck, and I’m an architect and urban planner who graduated from the Federal University of Minas Gerais (UFMG) in Brazil. I’m currently pursuing a Master of Science in Urban Spatial Science at the Bartlett Centre for Advanced Spatial Analysis (CASA), University College London (UCL).
This page is my learning diary for the module Remotely Sensing Cities and Environments. Here, I’ll be sharing weekly posts about the course content and my reflections along the way. I hope you enjoy following this journey!

title: 1  Week 01: Getting started with remote sensing
text:
1.1 Summary
Remote sensing can be defined as the acquisition of information about the Earth from a distance. To collect Earth observation data, sensors are required, which can be classified as either passive or active. Passive sensors rely on naturally available energy, such as sunlight, and include instruments like the human eye, cameras, and optical satellite sensors. In contrast, active sensors provide their own source of energy for illumination, as seen in systems such as Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR).
Both types of sensors operate using electromagnetic (EM) waves, which are generated by vibrating charged particles and travel through the vacuum of space at the constant speed of light. EM waves vary in wavelength and frequency: as frequency and energy increase, wavelength decreases. For example, gamma rays have very short wavelengths and high frequencies, whereas radio waves have long wavelengths and low frequencies. Although all EM waves travel at the same speed, only a small portion of the spectrum is visible to the human eye. Objects appear coloured because they absorb certain wavelengths of visible light and reflect others, with the reflected wavelengths determining the observed colour.
Importantly, all materials on Earth emit, absorb, and reflect EM radiation differently. Understanding these interactions allows us to study environmental phenomena such as vegetation dynamics, seasonal change, surface temperature, material composition, and habitat distribution. When EM energy reaches the Earth’s surface, it is partially absorbed and reflected. Before reaching the sensor, the reflected energy may be scattered or absorbed by atmospheric particles. Factors such as scattering, clouds, polarisation, fluorescence, and the Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF) can significantly influence the recorded signal.
Shorter wavelengths scatter more easily in the atmosphere, explaining why the sky appears blue. Atmospheric scattering can also obstruct surface observations, particularly due to clouds. Active sensors using longer wavelengths can overcome this limitation, as they are capable of penetrating clouds and other atmospheric disturbances.
Finally, remotely sensed data are characterised by four key resolutions: spatial (pixel size), spectral (number of wavelength bands), temporal (revisit frequency), and radiometric (sensitivity to differences in energy). Environmental conditions and sensor limitations often constrain data choice, making it essential to select appropriate data for specific applications.
crumbs: 1  Week 01: Getting started with remote sensing

title: 1  Week 01: Getting started with remote sensing
text:
1.2 Application
Remote sensing is widely used to observe and monitor the Earth using spaceborne, airborne, and ground-based sensors, and many studies show how these different platforms are suited to different applications. For example, spaceborne sensors are commonly used to track changes in vegetation over large areas by analysing reflected near-infrared (NIR) radiation, which is strongly related to plant health. This approach is often discussed in the literature in relation to agriculture, deforestation, and seasonal ecosystem changes, as it allows patterns to be observed consistently over time. Airborne sensors, such as those mounted on drones or aircraft, are frequently used when more detailed information is needed, for instance in precision farming or forest management. Ground-based measurements are also important, as they are often used to validate and interpret remotely sensed data, helping to link satellite observations with real conditions on the ground. These examples highlight how practical constraints, such as spatial resolution and coverage, influence the choice of remote sensing data in real applications.
Remote sensing is also widely used in climate and environmental studies, especially to understand how energy moves between the Earth and the atmosphere. This is often described as the radiation budget, which refers to how much energy from the Sun reaches the Earth, how much is reflected back into space, and how much is absorbed and re-emitted as heat. Satellite data help researchers study these energy exchanges, which are important for understanding climate change and temperature patterns. Reflected near-infrared (NIR) waves are particularly useful because healthy vegetation reflects a large amount of NIR energy. By analysing this reflected energy, scientists can easily identify vegetation cover, plant stress, and seasonal changes across large areas. This makes NIR data very valuable for monitoring crops, forests, and ecosystems.
To make sense of the large amount of data collected by sensors, Digital Image Processing (DIP) techniques are used. These methods help clean the data by removing noise, correcting for atmospheric effects, and improving image quality. DIP also allows researchers to highlight specific features, compare images taken at different times, and extract useful information such as land-cover types or changes after extreme events. For example, processed satellite images are often used to map flooded areas, track wildfire damage, or study melting ice. Overall, these concepts show how remote sensing data are transformed into meaningful information that can support environmental studies, decision-making, and practical applications.
crumbs: 1  Week 01: Getting started with remote sensing

title: 1  Week 01: Getting started with remote sensing
text:
1.3 Reflection
Earth Observation (EO) data have strong potential to support environmental policy because they provide consistent, large-scale information about the Earth’s surface. However, integrating EO data into policy is not always straightforward, due to constraints such as limited technical expertise, challenges in interpreting satellite outputs, and gaps between scientific results and policy needs. One important aspect of EO is that different sensors can be selected according to environmental conditions; for example, radar data are often used in regions with frequent cloud cover where optical imagery is unreliable. This demonstrates that EO can be adapted to real-world problems, but only when its limitations are clearly understood. The increasing availability of free satellite data, such as imagery from the Sentinel and Landsat missions, together with online platforms like Google Earth Engine, has made EO more accessible to governments and organisations with limited technical resources. These tools allow users to analyse a wide range of surface changes, including deforestation, flooding, crop conditions, urban expansion, and ship movements, without the need to process raw data from scratch. As a result, EO-based evidence can inform planning decisions and support a more critical evaluation of policy claims based on spatial data.
crumbs: 1  Week 01: Getting started with remote sensing

title: 3  Week 03: Corrections
text:
3.1 Summary
Satellite images aims to capture the Earth’s surface, but sometimes it can contains flaws that need to be corrected in order to provide better pictures. There are four major types of corrections in remote sensing: geometric, atmospheric, orthorectification/topographic and radiometric.
Geometric correction is needed when image distortion occurs, normally due to view angle, topography, wind or rotation of the Earth. This correction is done by identifying Ground Control Points (GCP) to match known points in the image and a reference dataset. There are many algorithms that try to transform the image coordinates into a actual coordinates, and the model with lower RMSE will fit best.
Atmospheric correction is used when the atmosphere (air, dust, haze, water vapor, etc) in between the sensor and the observed object creates light effects in the picture. Moreover, there are three major types of atmospheric correction: relative, absolute and empirical line correction. First, relative correction adjusts images so they are consistent with each other (reducing differences caused by atmosphere or lighting) without converting to true physical reflectance values. Dark object subtraction (DOS) and Psuedo-invariant Features (PIFs) are examples of relative correction. Absolute correction uses physical models of the atmosphere and sensor to convert measured radiance into real surface reflectance values. And empirical line correction uses known ground targets with measured reflectance (like bright and dark surfaces) to directly calibrate the image to true reflectance.
Orthorectification correction or topographic correction is applied to remove distortions, created by the position of the satellite in relation to the Earth. Additionally, it is done by giving coordinates to the image and creating a nadir view (from strait down). Some methodologies can be used to do this correction, such as cosine correction, Minnaert correction, Statistical Empirical correction and C Correction.
Radiometric calibration is needed because sensor noise, instrument drift, varying illumination conditions, and atmospheric effects can distort the recorded signal, making images from different dates or sensors difficult to compare. Furthermore, this correction fixes how bright or dark satellite images appear so the numbers in the image truly represent how much light is coming from the Earth’s surface. This is done by converting raw image numbers into real light values (radiance and reflectance) and by removing atmospheric effects using simple or physical correction methods.
After analysing if any type of correction is needed, it might be necessary to join different satellite images. Additionally, when joining two satellite images together, they are made to overlap by about 20–30% so their brightness can be compared and adjusted. In the overlap area, a sample of pixels is taken and a brightness histogram from the base image is created, then this histogram is matched to the second image so both images have similar brightness levels. This helps remove visible differences between the images, and finally feathering is applied to smoothly blend the edges so the join looks natural and seamless.
Image enhancement adjusts features, such as brightness, contrast, and sharpness, to highlight some objects in the Earth’s surface (e.g. roads, vegetation, water, or land differences). Moreover, most materials reflect similar amounts of energy in the same wavelengths, and sensors are designed to avoid saturation, so images often use only a small part of the available brightness range. Contrast stretching expands this limited range to use more of the available values, and this can be done using methods such as minimum–maximum stretching, percentage linear stretching, standard deviation stretching, and piecewise linear stretching.

title: 3  Week 03: Corrections
text:
3.2 Application
Remote sensing corrections are widely used in environmental monitoring studies, especially when analysing changes over time. For example, atmospheric and radiometric corrections are essential in vegetation studies to monitor deforestation, drought stress, or crop productivity. Many studies using Landsat and Sentinel imagery apply surface reflectance products (such as Analysis Ready Data) to ensure that differences in vegetation health are caused by real land cover change rather than lighting conditions or haze. Without these corrections, seasonal comparisons could incorrectly suggest vegetation decline or improvement due to atmospheric effects instead of actual ecological change.
Geometric correction and orthorectification are also important in applications such as urban growth mapping and disaster monitoring. For instance, accurately aligned satellite images are needed to assess flood extent, earthquake damage, or land use expansion. Studies often rely on GCP-based correction and digital elevation models to ensure spatial accuracy, particularly in mountainous regions where terrain distortion is strong. Image mosaicking techniques with histogram matching and feathering are commonly used to create large regional maps from multiple scenes, such as national land cover datasets. This shows how correction methods directly support practical decision-making in environmental management and planning.

title: 3  Week 03: Corrections
text:
3.3 Reflection
Pre-processing is required before satellite images can be used for analysis, and although many modern datasets are provided as corrected products such as Analysis Ready Data (ARD), this will not always be the case. In that sense, it is important to know how to handle data that requires manual correction, such as very high-resolution imagery or drone data. It also became clear that receiving a dataset does not guarantee its reliability, as the correction methods applied can strongly influence the final results. This encouraged a more critical approach toward corrected imagery and raised questions about how much corrected data can truly be trusted. Overall, learning about the range of correction techniques showed that there is no single best method, and that selecting the most appropriate approach depends on the study context, data type, and research objectives.

title: 4  Week 04: Policy
text:
4.1 Summary
The Belo Horizonte’s Plan, also called ‘Plano Diretor’, was established by Municipal Law 11,181 in 2019. This policy aims to address issues related to urban structure, urban development, environment, housing, cultural and urban heritage, and mobility, as well as the treatment of public and private spaces in Belo Horizonte, capital of the state of Minas Gerais in Brazil. One thing that this plan does is define land use zones for the city, and for each zone there are specific urban parameters to guide land use and urban expansion. Moreover, there are ten types of zones, and among them is the environmental preservation zone, which is subdivided into three levels (PA1, PA2 and PA3), from the most to the least restrictive in terms of land use.
knitr::include_graphics('img/bh_pd_zoneamento.png')
Source: Secretaria Municipal de Política Urbana da Prefeitura de Belo Horizonte (2020) E-book Plano Diretor de BH: OCUPAÇÃO DO SOLO”. Available at: https://prefeitura.pbh.gov.br/sites/default/files/estrutura-de-governo/politica-urbana/2023/001_zoneamento_0.pdf (Accessed: 22 February 2026).
Environmental preservation zones were established in areas where there are environmental attributes that need to be preserved, such as springs, watercourses, valley bottoms and dense fragments of native vegetation. Finally, the way to ensure that this policy is respected is by requiring a municipal licence to build and clear land in these areas, where the technical staff of the local council assesses whether the parameters established by law are being complied with. However, this mechanism does not cover informal actions, which are currently only identified through complaints and on-site inspections.

title: 4  Week 04: Policy
text:
4.2 Application
Considering that Belo Horizonte has an area of over 330,000km² and population of 2,315,560, according to the 2022 Census, the identification of informal land use solely through the methods currently in place is insufficient and/or too costly to cover the entire municipal territory satisfactorily. One way to make this process more efficient is through the application of Earth Observation (EO) to extract and process data from satellites and airborne imagery. This data driven strategy can be used to measure and monitor unsustainable and irregular urban development, providing spatial and temporal scales that are necessary to inform municipalities actions (MacLachlan, Biggs, Roberts, and Boruff, 2021).
Sentinel-2 would be a good satellite option for monitoring environmental preservation zones in Belo Horizonte, due to its resolution, cost and suitability in this context. Its high-resolution technology (10m resolution for visible and near-infrared bands) is ideal for identifying small clearings or gradual occupation, before large-scale degradation occurs. Additionally, it is open-access imagery with no licensing costs. Moreover, data from Sentinel-2 can be processed by QGIS, which is open source software, and Google Earth Engine, which is free for research and/or the public sector. Furthermore, the revisit time for this satellite is 5 days, ensuring constant monitoring, and it enables temporal comparison from 2015 to the present. Finally, it has 13 spectral bands, including red-edge bands specifically designed for vegetation analysis and supervision of riparian zones and water bodies.

title: 4  Week 04: Policy
text:
4.3 Reflection
In addition to aligning with the definitions of the Belo Horizonte’s Plan (municipal scale), the application of Earth Observation for monitoring unsustainable and irregular urban development is in line with the UN Sustainable Development Goals (global scale). Specifically, Goal 11: ‘Make cities and human settlements inclusive, safe, resilient and sustainable’, which advocates for sustainable urbanisation and strengthening efforts to protect the world’s natural heritage, respectively in sub-items 11.3 and 11.4. In addition, it is also aligned with Goals 13 and 15, respectively: ‘Take urgent action to combat climate change and its impacts’ and ‘Protect, restore and promote sustainable use of terrestrial ecosystems, sustainably manage forests, combat desertification, and halt and reverse land degradation and halt biodiversity loss’. Overall, it is understood that the use of Sentinel-2 would increase the effectiveness of Belo Horizonte’s plan, contributing to the protection of the local environment. However, this technology would also help to monitor unregulated urban sprawl, preventing the formation of new settlements where there is no urban infrastructure (clean water, sewage collection, drainage, public lighting and paving). Overall, remote sensing might be a powerful tool to inform policies in different scales.

title: 5  Week 06: An introduction to Google Earth Engine
text:
5.1 Summary
Google Earth Engine (GEE) is a platform/tool powered by Google’s Data Center Infrastructure to process and analyse geospatial data. Moreover, it’s cloud-service has a great volume of remote sensing data, including Landsat, Sentinel 1 and 2, MODIS, and no-satellite imagery, which allows users to do quickly planetary scale analysis. The data in GEE is organised in object classes, such as Image (raster), ImageCollection, Geometry (vector), Feature (vector with atributes), FeatureCollection, Reducer, Join, Array and Chart. Furthermore, for each kind of class there are specific methods to select and manipulate data.
Beyond data analysis, this platform enable researchers to do geometry operations, such as joins, zonal statistics filtering, and create artificial intelligence models (machine learning and deep learning) based on the available data. Another GEE’s feature is reducing images, which allows users to create a “summary image” from a ImageCollection (raster’s stack), using the median value for each pixel from the collection. Reduction can be done by region(s), when the reduction occurs for a specific boundary of the study area, and users want to extract a statistic of this place (minimum, mean, maximum, etc). It can also be done by neighbourhood, what means that it will reduce the information in a small window/kernel to a single value for the center pixel. Finally, a different way to reduce images in GEE is using regression. This method allows analysing how values change over time, producing two kinds of images: the intercept (representing the starting value) and the slope (representing the speed or rate of change over time).
crumbs: 5  Week 06: An introduction to Google Earth Engine

title: 5  Week 06: An introduction to Google Earth Engine
text:
5.2 Application
Google Earth Engine (GEE) is a “game-changer” because it allows planetary-scale analysis that was previously impossible for most researchers. One famous application is the work by Hansen et al. (2013), who mapped global forest change over a decade. By using linear regressions and decision trees on over 600,000 Landsat scenes, they completed a massive processing task in just 100 hours, something that would have taken much more time in a traditional computer. This demonstrates how GEE’s ability to “reduce” a large amount of images into maps of “loss” and “gain” provides vital information for biodiversity and climate conservation.

knitr::include_graphics('img/wk6_f1.jpeg')








“(A) Tree cover, (B) forest loss, and (C) forest gain. A color composite of tree cover in green, forest loss in red, forest gain in blue, and forest loss and gain in magenta is shown in (D), with loss and gain enhanced for improved visualization. All map layers have been resampled for display purposes from the 30-m observation scale to a 0.05° geographic grid.” Source: Hansen et al. (2013)
Beyond forests, GEE is also used in agriculture and urban planning to help achieve global development goals. For instance, Lobell et al. (2015) used GEE to create a scalable crop yield (agricultural production) mapper that processed millions of hectares of maize and soy fields. In urban studies, researchers like Ravanelli et al. (2018) have used GEE to monitor surface urban heat islands. By using reducers to analyse twenty years of temperature data, they were able to show a direct link between urban growth and rising heat levels in major cities. These studies show that GEE’s data catalog is a powerful tool for monitoring how earth surface is changing.

knitr::include_graphics('img/wk6_f2.jpeg')








“Overview of the study area in the Midwestern United States. State (thick white lines) and county (thin white lines) boundaries are shown for the three states where yield estimates were made: Iowa, Illinois, and Indiana. Black boxes indicate regions for which detailed comparisons with ground-based yield records were made for 2008–2012.” Source: Lobell et al. (2015)

knitr::include_graphics('img/wk6_f3.png')








“Atlanta MA: LC situation in 1992 (a) and in 2011 (b). In red, the Urbanized class, in light green, the Cultivated class, in dark green, the Forest and Shrubland classes.” Source: Ravanelli et al. (2018)
crumbs: 5  Week 06: An introduction to Google Earth Engine

title: 5  Week 06: An introduction to Google Earth Engine
text:
5.3 Reflection
Google Earth Engine represents the democratization of big data. Before GEE, only elite scientists with expensive supercomputers could handle large amounts of satellite imagery because of technology challenges, such as managing storage and obscure file formats. This platform handles those issues automatically through its cloud-based infrastructure, allowing anyone with an internet connection to perform complex science. Moreover, the concept of reducers is particularly powerful. It enables users to summarise a 30-year stack of data into a regression slope that shows environmental and/or urban trends, which could be very useful in future works. While there are still some challenges, such as “time-out” errors when a task is too complex, GEE changes the focus from the technical struggle of handling data to the creative process of answering research questions.
crumbs: 5  Week 06: An introduction to Google Earth Engine

title: 6  Week 07: Classification I
text:
6.1 Summary
This week is about classification, and how Earth Observation data can be analysed and classified under expert systems and machine learning lenses. To start with, expert system is a computer program designed to copy the way a human expert thinks to solve a problem. In this context, it works by using two main parts: knowledge base and inference engine. The knowledge base is a set of rules and/or logic provided by humans experts, and the inference engine is the part of the system that looks at the rules in the knowledge base to reach a conclusion. Here, it is important to notice that, as expert systems relies in human’s rules it is not imune to bias and errors. In addition, machine learning is when the computer learn from itself (without human’s coded-rules) by analysing the data. In other words, the system searches for patterns in the data (creating it’s own rules) to make conclusions, and this is called inductive learning.That said, the use of inductive learning to classify Earth Observation Data and identify land cover patterns have been used to analyse different contexts, such as urban expansion, air pollution, urban green spaces, forest fires, monitoring forests and illegal logging.
Some machine learning methods are: linear regression, classification and regression trees (CART), random forests and support vector machines (SVM). Linear regression fits a mathematical model to existing data so that it can be used to make predictions about new data. Decision trees split data into smaller and more similar groups, and there are two kinds of decision trees: classification and regression ones. Classification trees are used to sort data into discrete categories, such as deciding if a pixel in a satellite image is “forest” or “water”. And regression trees are used to predict continuous numeric values, for example the specific amount of air pollution in a certain area. Random forests are sets of many different decision trees working together. It is usually more accurate then single decision trees because, while a single decision tree might struggle with new data, a forest of trees provides a more robust and less overfitted classification through a “majority vote” system. Support Vector Machines (SVM) aim to find the best way to divide the data in two groups. Additionally, there are two main concepts behind SVM. The first one is the hyperplane, which means the “line” used to divide the data. And the second one is the margin, which means the buffer zone or empty space on either side of the line. Moreover, in SVM the margin should be as big as possible and it is a machine learning method that works well even if the model has a small number of training samples.
Despite their strengths, these methods have also limitations. Individual decision trees are highly susceptible to overfitting, where the model becomes so specific to the training pixels that it fails to accurately classify new, unseen data (Jensen, 2015). While Random Forests and SVMs are more robust, they are often criticised as “black boxes” because it can be difficult to see exactly how the computer reached its final conclusion (Sheykhmousa et al., 2020). Finally, no classification is 100% accurate because they are only as good as the information we give them. In other words, if the starting information has any degree of uncertainty, the final result will reflect that (GISGeography, 2014; Barsi et al., 2018). For example, atmospheric interference and shadows can distort spectral signatures, and even the human-provided training samples might contain errors that the computer then unintentionally replicates (Barsi et al., 2018).

title: 6  Week 07: Classification I
text:
6.2 Application
One example of imagery classification is the Hungarian government’s use of remote sensing to manage agricultural subsidies. In Hungary, the government uses a digital map system called the Land Parcel Identification System (LPIS) to check on farmers who ask for financial help. Instead of sending people to visit every farm, they use high-quality photos from planes and satellites to see exactly what is growing on the land. This approach is faster and more objective to ensure the right people get the correct amount of money (Barsi et al., 2018). The paper explains that this is done through visual interpretation, where experts look at high-quality aerial photos to draw lines around fields and identify crops. This is part of a larger government system called the Integrated Administration and Control System (IACS), which uses these images to check at least 5% of all farm claims every year to prevent mistakes or fraud (Barsi et al., 2018).
A second example is the study of Support Vector Machines (SVM) used to map different crops in places like Littleport, England, and La Mancha, Spain. Researchers discovered that this method is excellent at identifying specific crops like wheat, potatoes, and vineyards, even without a lot of starting data (Pal and Mather, 2005). To achieve this, the paper tested two specific ways for the computer to sort many different categories: “one-against-one” and “one-against-the-rest.” They found that the “one-against-one” method was both faster and more accurate. They also used a mathematical tool called a Radial Basis Kernel Function to help the computer find the best “lines” (hyperplanes) to separate complex data, such as different types of vegetation that look very similar from a distance (Pal and Mather, 2005).
When comparing these two papers, they show different but connected ways to use remote sensing. The work by Barsi et al. (2018) describes a massive administrative system used for national policy, where humans are still very involved in looking at the images to make legal decisions about money. In contrast, the study by Pal and Mather (2005) is about testing how automated algorithms like SVM can do the sorting work on their own. Essentially, while Barsi et al. (2018) focus on how a country uses the data to follow European Union rules, Pal and Mather (2005) use modern machine learning methods to support classification in remote sensing.

title: 6  Week 07: Classification I
text:
6.3 Reflection
This week, I gained a deeper understanding of how to transform raw satellite imagery into thematic maps through supervised and unsupervised classification techniques. Learning the practical workflow in Google Earth Engine, from masking clouds, using Sentinel-2 quality assurance (QA) bands, to training machine learning models like Random Forest and Support Vector Machines (SVM) showed me that classification might provide a balance between data-driven math and human expertise. I also realised that no classification is 100% perfect due to inherent data uncertainties. These skills will be valuable for my future work in urban planning and sustainable development, as they provide the evidence needed to monitor compliance with global agendas like the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). For example, I could use these methods to track urban expansion over decades or analyse the relationship between land-use changes and deforestation. Also, considering city-scale analysis, it is important to understand that Object-Based Image Analysis (OBIA) is superior for high-resolution urban data, because it groups pixels into meaningful shapes like buildings or parks. In the future, I aim to integrate these Earth Observation workflows into city data pipelines to help planners make informed, sustainable decisions regarding green space management and urban growth.

title: 6  Week 07: Classification I
text:
6.4 References
MacLachlan, A. CASA0023 Remotely Sensing Cities and Environments: 6 Classification I. Available at: https://andrewmaclachlan.github.io/CASA0023/6_classification_I.html (Accessed: 06 March 2026).
Jensen, J.R. (2015) Introductory digital image processing: a remote sensing perspective. 4th edn. Glenview, IL: Prentice-Hall Inc.
Sheykhmousa, M., Mahdianpari, M., Ghanbari, H., Mohammadimanesh, F., Ghamisi, P. and Homayouni, S.
